segments.s091-g41: تن پهلوان را نیارم به رنج = literal order: direct object (تن پهلوان را) + verb phrase. تن پهلوان را is the definite object 'the hero's body' (تن = body, پهلوان = champion; را marks it as object). نیارم here is a poetic/colloquial contracted form of نیاورم ('I will not bring' / 'I shall not cause'), and به رنج attaches the preposition به + noun رنج 'to / into suffering' showing the result/state to be avoided.
segments.s091-g42: فرستمش هرگونه آگنده گنج = main verb clause where فرستمش (فرستم + -ش enclitic) means 'I will send him/it'; هرگونه is an adverbial 'in any way / by any means'; آگنده is an adjective 'laden/filled' modifying the following noun گنج 'treasure'. Word order is Verb + adverbial + adjective + noun, with the enclitic -ش referring back to the hero/person mentioned earlier.
segments.s091-g43: ازین سو دل پهلوان را ببست = 'ازین سو' ('from this side') is a prepositional/locative phrase (از + این/این spelled ین here + سو 'side'); دل پهلوان را is the object 'the hero's heart'; ببست (past) is 'he bound/tied' here used transitively 'he bound/fastened the heart' (i.e., fixed or restrained the heart). The clause follows the typical Persian order: adverbial/place → object → finite verb.
segments.s091-g44: وزان در سوی چاره یازید دست = وزان ('and from that / and thence') links to the previous clause; در here functions as 'door / opening' (not the preposition), so 'در سوی' = 'at/near that door toward/for'; چاره is 'remedy/solution'; یازید (from یازیدن) is a past tense 'opened' or 'stretched/opened' and دست ('hand') is the object meaning 'hand/arm' — together 'he opened/stretched his hand toward a remedy.'
segments.s091-g45: نوندی برافگند نزدیک زال = نوندی is here treated as a proper name or special term (a nominative actor) and is followed by the verb برافگند 'threw off / cast aside / dispelled'; نزدیک زال places the action 'near Zal.' Word order: subject (نوندی) → verb (برافگند) → locative phrase (نزدیک زال).
segments.s091-g46: که پرنده شو باز کن پر و بال = که introduces reported speech or a purpose/command clause ('saying that'); پرنده شو is an imperative/hortative sequence 'become a bird' (پرنده = bird, شو = become — imperative form); باز کن is an imperative 'open / spread' (باز here = 'open'), and پر و بال 'feathers and wings' are the objects of that opening. The clause has the imperative verbs applying to the addressee.
segments.s091-g47: به دستان بگو آنچ دیدی ز کار = به + Dative phrase, 'to Dastān (or to the hands)', بگو is the imperative 'say / tell'; آنچ (آنچه) = 'that which / what' and دیدی = 'you saw' (past); ز کار = 'of the affair / regarding the deed' (ز = from/of + کار = affair). The whole segment is a command: 'tell (to) Dastān what you saw of the matter.'
segments.s091-g48: بگویش که از آمدن سر مخار = بگویش is 'tell him' (بگو + enclitic -ش); که introduces the content clause 'that ...'; از آمدن is a prepositional phrase 'about/from the coming' (از + آمدن); سر مخار is an idiomatic/archaic phrase — سر is 'head/top' but here combines with مخار (an uncertain/rare word) and likely forms an admonition or comment whose precise meaning is obscure. Grammatically, the clause is 'tell him that (concerning) the coming ... (سر مخار).'
segments.s091-g1: This is a subordinating clause introduced by و (and) plus دیگر که (literally 'then/after that when') giving the time of the action. از شهر ارمان is a simple prepositional phrase 'from the city of Arman' (preposition از before the noun), and شدند is the past plural of شدن used as the main past-tense verb 'they left/they departed' in poetic diction. The whole segment functions as a temporal subordinate clause ('when they departed from the city of Arman').
segments.s091-g2: This main clause follows the temporal clause. به کینه is an adverbial phrase 'with hatred/with a grudge' (به marking the manner), سوی زابلستان is a directional phrase 'toward Zabulistan' (سوی + place), and شدند is the past plural verb 'they went/they set out'. Word order is typical Persian: manner/place phrases before the verb, and the verb (شدند) closes the clause.
segments.s091-g3: شماساس is the head noun 'the sexton' and kز + پیش + جیحون forms a relative/locative phrase 'who/which from before/beyond the Jeyhun'. برفت is the past of رفتن 'he went/left'. The clause is a relative clause modifying شماساس: 'the sexton who had come from beyond the Jeyhun went.' Note: some words and forms are poetically compressed here.
segments.s091-g4: سوی سیستان is a directional phrase 'toward Sistan'. روی بنهاد is a light-verb construction: روی (face) + بنهاد (past of نهادن 'to place/set') = 'he set/turned his face (toward)'; this is a common Persian idiom meaning 'he turned/turned his face toward'. و تفت attaches another item (likely a place-name) with و 'and'. The verb implied by بنهاد governs both directions.
segments.s091-g5: This nominal group lists a troop or host: خزروان is a collective/title (here 'Khazravans' or 'kings/royalty') followed by a name or epithet ابا تیغ‌زن (Aba, the sword-striker). The number phrase 'سی هزار' (thirty thousand) is apposed to the group and indicates the size; Persian poetic order allows apposition and epithets to be arranged for meter. The whole string names the force and its leader/characteristic.
segments.s091-g6: ز ترکان is a genitive/of‑phrase in poetic form meaning 'from the Turks' (ز as a poetic variant of از). بزرگان is an epithet 'the great ones' or 'nobles', and خنجرگزار is a compound noun 'dagger-bearers' (those who carry daggers), here qualifying those Turks. The sequence gives origin + rank + characteristic: 'from the Turks, noble men, dagger‑bearers.'
segments.s091-g7: برفتند is the past plural verb 'they went/they set out'. بیدار is an adverb/adjective 'awake/alert' describing their state. تا هیرمند uses تا in the spatial sense 'as far as' followed by the place-name هیرمند (Helmand); together they indicate the extent of their advance: 'they went, alert, as far as the Helmand.'
segments.s091-g8: This is a coordinated instrumental phrase listing what they bore: (ابا) تیغ و با گرز و بخت بلند. با ... (با گرز) is 'with ... and with ...' marking instruments 'with sword and with mace'. بخت بلند is a nominal phrase 'high/lofty fortune' (بخت = fortune, بلند = high) describing their auspicious lot. Note: the initial ابا is a poetic/orthographic variant here and functions like با 'with' in context; the clause as a whole modifies their march (they were armed and fortunate).
segments.s091-g25: ازایدر چو is a temporal clause: ازایدر (“from here/this place”) + چو (“when”) introduces the time. دستان is the subject (classical plural/collective ‘tales, stories’), بشد is the past-form verb ‘became/was made’ (بـ-شد used as perfective/past), and سوگوار is a predicate adjective ‘mournful/lamenting’. Word order is topicalized (place + temporal marker) then subject + verb + predicative adjective, typical of epic Persian.
segments.s091-g26: ز بهر is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning ‘for the sake of/for’ (ز used in the sense ‘because of/for’ and بهر ‘for’). ستودان is the infinitive/masdar ‘to praise’ (used as a noun: ‘praising’), and سام سوار is a noun phrase in apposition (Sam, the rider/horseman). Together the phrase means ‘for praising Sam the rider’. Note the poetic order: the purpose/benefit phrase follows the temporal clause.
segments.s091-g27: دلم is دل + the possessive suffix -م (‘my heart’). شادمان شد is a predication: شادمان ‘glad’ as predicate adjective and شد ‘became’ (past). به تیمار اوی is a prepositional phrase ‘because of/by his care’ (به + تیمار ‘care/treatment’, with اوی the classical possessive pronoun ‘his’ placed after the noun).
segments.s091-g28: برآنم is a fixed clause meaning ‘I am resolved / I intend’ (first-person); که introduces the content clause (‘that …’). هرگز is the negative adverb ‘never’. نبینمش is composed of the negative prefix ن + verb بین (to see) in the first-person form نبینم + enclitic object -ش ‘him’, so ‘I (shall) not see him’. روی (face) is the direct object placed after the verb. The whole clause expresses a resolute intention not to see his face.
segments.s091-g29: This is an object-fronted construction: زمان (‘time’) is fronted as the thing desired, followed by خواهم (first-person ‘I will/want’), then the source/agent introduced by از نامور پهلوان (‘from/of the renowned champion’). In plain order it reads ‘I will want/ask time from the famed champion.’ خواهم here carries a future/intention meaning.
segments.s091-g30: بدان is an archaic/deictic combination (ب- as prepositional prefix + دان) used here as ‘to/unto him/thereupon’ (a purpose/recipient link); تا introduces a purpose clause ‘so that/in order that’. فرستم is first-person singular (I send/will send), and هیونی دوان is a noun + participle/adjective: هیونی ‘beast/animal’ modified by دوان (‘running, galloping’), i.e. ‘a rushing/fast beast’. So the clause means ‘to him so that I send a swift beast.’
segments.s091-g31: یکی here functions as an indefinite/article ‘a certain’ (not strictly the numeral); مرد is the head noun ‘man’. بینادل is a rare/uncertain lexeme in the manuscript tradition (likely an epithet; its precise sense is uncertain but functions adjectivally), و connects adjectives, and پرشتاب is an adjective ‘rapid/very swift’. The phrase lists the man and his characteristic qualities—‘a certain (بینادل) and swift man.’
segments.s091-g32: فرستم is first-person ‘I will send’ (present/future or subjunctive with volitional sense). به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase ‘to the vicinity/near to’, where نزدیک functions nominally as ‘near/nearby’, and افراسیاب is the proper name (Afrasiab). Word order places the verb before the destination phrase, normal in poetry.
segments.s091-g17: که here is the relative/complementizer 'that/who' introducing the clause. بیداردل is an adjectival epithet 'wakeful‑hearted' placed before شاه, qualifying it; شاه توران is a genitive noun phrase 'the king of Turan' (توران postmodifies شاه). سپاه is the head noun 'army/host' that will be linked to the verb in the next segment.
segments.s091-g18: بماناد is an optative/jussive 3rd‑person form meaning 'may ... remain' (archaic/poetic). تا here functions as a temporal 'until' giving duration, so تا جاودان = 'until eternal' → 'forever'. با is the preposition 'with' and کلاه means 'helmet/hat' (here plural/collective in sense). The verb governs the preceding noun phrase (the king of Turan's army) as its subject.
segments.s091-g19: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. ضحاک is the proper name Zahhak. تازیست is a predicative compound (تازی + است) meaning 'is Tazi/Arab' (third‑person). ما را نژاد is 'our lineage/stock' where را marks/flags the phrase; word order is inverted for emphasis: literally 'from Zahhak Arab is our race.'
segments.s091-g20: بدین is the fused prepositional/demonstrative element 'in/with this' (ب + دین in older usage) introducing the circumstance 'in this kingship' (پادشاهی). نیم سخت is a compound adverbial phrase 'half/partly + strongly/firmly', so نیم سخت شاد qualifies the speaker's state: 'somewhat/partly strongly glad' → 'half‑firmly glad' (i.e. not wholly content). شاد is the predicate 'happy/glad.'
segments.s091-g21: به پیوستگی is an instrumental phrase 'by/through connectedness/steadfastness' (به + noun). جان is the object 'life/soul' of خریدم, which is the simple past 1st‑person 'I bought/gained.' همی is an archaic particle here used as an emphatic or continuous marker ('indeed' / 'thus').
segments.s091-g22: جز این نیز is a concessive phrase 'other than this / apart from this (also)' with نیز adding emphasis. چاره is 'remedy/solution' and ندیدم is the past 1st‑person 'I did not see/found no' (negated perception = 'I saw no'). همی again adds archaic emphasis. Together: 'I saw no remedy besides this.'
segments.s091-g23: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. این سرای و نشست is a coordinated noun phrase 'this house (سرای) and seat/residence (نشست)'—نشست here means dwelling/place of sitting. منست is an archaic contracted predicate ('man‑ast' = 'is mine' / 'belongs to me') stating possession; word order places the predicate after the noun phrase as usual in Persian.
segments.s091-g24: همان is the demonstrative 'that very/the same'. زاولستان is a place name. به دست means 'into the hand/under the power of' where به is the preposition 'to/under' and دست carries the idiomatic sense 'power/control.' منست repeats the archaic 'is mine/is under my control.' The clause reiterates that the named district lies under the speaker's authority.
segments.s091-g33: مگر introduces an exception/conditional meaning 'unless'. کز is the archaic contracted relative/conjunction (که + ز) linking to نهان من 'my secret' as its source/thing; نهان من is a genitive phrase 'my secret/hidden (thing)'. آگه شود is a compound of آگه (aware) + شود (become) and expresses 'become aware' (present/subjunctive/passive-style verb phrase in Persian narrative style).
segments.s091-g34: سخنهای گوینده is a nominal phrase 'the speaker's words' (سخنهای = words/speeches + possessive relation to گوینده). کوته شود is the predicative verb phrase 'become short / be cut short' where شود is the ordinary stative/becoming verb; the whole clause states the result if the condition fails/is not met.
segments.s091-g35: نثاری (a noun/adjective) means 'a prose (message)' and is the direct object of فرستم. فرستم is first-person present/subjunctive used to indicate sending (future/intentional sense 'I will send'). چنان چون سزاست is a fixed formula: چنان = 'such/so', چون = 'as/like', and سزاست is a predicate 'it is fitting/merited', so the phrase means 'in such a way as is proper.'
segments.s091-g36: جز این نیز opens with 'except/aside from this' (جز این). هرچ is an indefinite 'whatever/anything that', and از در پادشاست is an archaic locative phrase 'from/in the presence of the king / from the king's court' where در functions as 'in/at (the court/gate)' and پادشاست denotes the royal authority/court. The clause thus limits the previous action and introduces anything else coming from the royal court.
segments.s091-g37: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/discourse particle meaning roughly 'then/so' (it frames the reported speech). گوید here functions as the verb 'he says' (reporting speech) though the provided sense list did not include that verbal sense. به نزد من آی is direct reported speech: به نزد = 'to the presence of', من = 'me', and آی is the imperative 'come'.
segments.s091-g38: جز ... نباشم is an idiomatic construction meaning 'I shall be nowhere except / I shall be nothing but ...'. از پیش تختش is a spatial phrase 'before his throne' (از + پیش used here to mean 'in front of / in the presence of'). نباشم is first-person present negative of بودن but here in combination with جز gives the exclusive sense 'I will be only ...'. به پای ('at [his] feet') further specifies the locus.
segments.s091-g39: همه پادشاهی is the object phrase 'the whole kingdom'. سپارم is first‑person present/subjunctive of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over' (here indicating the speaker's intention: 'I will entrust'). بدوی is an archaic/poetic particle or vocative appended to the clause (its exact meaning is uncertain in isolation and treated here as an old/formulaic element).
segments.s091-g40: همیشه is 'always'. دلی شاد دارم is a compact predicate: دلی شاد = 'a joyful heart' (deli as adjectival/possessive form) and دارم = 'I have' (present). Again بدوی closes the line as an archaic/formulaic particle or vocative; its precise lexical sense here is unclear, so it is left as such.
segments.s091-g49: که is the narrative/relative conjunction 'that' linking this clause to what came before. دو پهلوان is numeral + noun ('two champions'); آمد is past tense 3rd-person singular 'came'. ایدر is an archaic/poetic locative 'here'. بجنگ is an infinitival/ purposive form ('to fight' / 'for battle') rather than an imperative; the verb آمد is followed by this phrase to express purpose/place: 'came here to fight.'
segments.s091-g50: ز marks origin/possession in poetic register ('of/from the Turks'), so ز ترکان سپاهی reads 'a troop/warriors of the Turks' (poetic word order without an explicit ezāfe). چو is the simile particle 'like/as'; دشتی is an adjectival epithet 'of the plain' modifying پلنگ 'leopard'. The whole segment likens that Turkish force to 'a leopard of the plain.'
segments.s091-g51: دو لشکر is 'two armies' (numeral before noun). کشیدند is past 3rd-person plural 'they drew/moved/drew up' — here used idiomatically to mean 'marched' or 'advanced'. بر is the preposition 'against' (movement toward/against), and هیرمند is the proper name Helmand. Note poetic order: verb precedes the prepositional phrase.
segments.s091-g52: به دینارشان: به marks goal/association 'to/for', دینارشان is 'their dinars' (possessive -شان). پای کردم is literal 'I made/put (a) foot' — an idiomatic poetic formula meaning 'I placed/anchored (as a base or note)' with کردم the 1st-person past 'I did/made'. به بند: the second به here has an 'into/toward' sense and بند is 'stanza' — together meaning the poet attached/placed their dinars in a stanza (i.e. made them the subject of a couplet).
segments.s091-g53: گر is the conditional 'if'. از آمدن 'of/about the coming' uses از to mark the topical source. دم زنی is a verbal phrase from دم زدن 'to utter/mention' — here the form زنی functions as a present/hypothetical 'you utter/you speak' (note: the provided dictionary senses for the string زنی are nouns; none match the verbal use, so mark that below). یک زمان 'one moment' is a temporal expression placed at the end (i.e. 'even for a moment').
segments.s091-g54: برآید is 3rd-person singular present/future-like 'will arise/appear'. همی is an emphatic adverb ('indeed/straightaway' in classical usage). کامه is the noun 'desire/wish' and بدگمان is an adjective 'suspicious/ill‑suspecting' placed after the noun as an epithet; together 'کامه بدگمان' = 'the desire of the suspicious one' or 'suspicious desire'.
segments.s091-g9: ز بهر is a poetic prepositional phrase 'for the sake of / because of' (ز + بهر) that introduces the beneficiary/cause; پدر زال is a noun phrase 'the father Zal' (possessor/name follows the noun in this older style). با + noun(s) 'با سوگ و درد' is a simple instrumental/attendant phrase 'with mourning and sorrow', where و links the two nouns.
segments.s091-g10: به ... اندر marks a locative direction: 'to/into (the) Gūrāb, inside' (به + place + اندر). همی is a little poetic auxiliary/particle attached to the verb complex (here adding emphasis/continuity). دخمه کرد is a verb phrase 'made/caused a tomb' (دخمه 'tomb' as object, کرد 'did/made' as main verb).
segments.s091-g11: به شهر اندرون is a locative phrase 'in/within the city'; گرد before مهراب functions here as a spatial/associative particle 'about/around' (classical placement) and مهراب بود is the main clause 'there was a mihrab' with بود as the past copula.
segments.s091-g12: که introduces a relative clause describing the mihrab ('which'); روشن‌روان is a compound adjective 'bright‑souled / radiant in spirit' and بود repeats the past copula; و links the two adjectival predicates, and بی‌خواب بود states the second predicate 'was sleepless.'
segments.s091-g13: فرستاده‌ای is an indefinite noun phrase 'a messenger' (the -ای here signals an indefinite/one who was sent); آمد is the past verb 'came.' از نزد اوی is a classical locative expression 'from his presence/near him' (نزد = in the presence of; اوی = archaic 3rd‑person pronoun).
segments.s091-g14: به سوی is the directional preposition 'toward'; شماساس is the addressee's name/title (a sexton). بنهاد روی is a light‑verb construction 'put/laid (his) face' i.e. 'turned his face toward'—بنهاد (past of نهاد/بنهان) = 'placed/put' and روی = 'face' as the object.
segments.s091-g15: به پیش marks motion 'to before/in front of'; سراپرده is 'the inner curtain/inner chamber' (the place reached). آمد فرود is an inverted verbal phrase (poetic order) equivalent to فرود آمد 'he descended / came down'—آمد (past 'came') + فرود (descent/alighting).
segments.s091-g16: ز مهراب = 'from the mihrab' (ز = from, poetic locative); دادش is داد + enclitic ش 'gave to him' (direct object pronoun attached); فراوان درود is the object 'abundant greeting(s)' — word order is Verb (دادش) + object phrase (فراوان درود) but here the giver (مهراب) is placed in front.

sentences.s091-s21: I will not bring the hero's body into suffering; I will send him, in any way, laden with treasure.
sentences.s091-s22: From this side he bound the hero's heart, and from that door he stretched out a hand toward finding a remedy.
sentences.s091-s23: Navand threw off (or cast aside) nearby Zal, saying, 'Become a bird; open your feathers and wings.'
sentences.s091-s24: Tell Dastān (or 'tell the Dastān') what you saw of the affair, and tell him that, regarding the coming, سر مخار.
sentences.s091-s1: And then, when they left the city of Arman, they set out for Zabulistan with hatred.
sentences.s091-s2: A sexton who had come from beyond the Jeyhun set out; he turned his face toward Sistan and Taft.
sentences.s091-s3: There were thirty thousand Khazravans — Aba the sword-wielder — from the Turks, noble men, dagger-bearers.
sentences.s091-s4: They marched on, alert, as far as the Helmand, with swords and with maces and with lofty fortune.
sentences.s091-s13: When the stories from here became mournful, it was for praising Sam the rider.
sentences.s091-s14: My heart became glad at his care; I am resolved that I will never see his face.
sentences.s091-s15: I will ask the famed champion for time, so that I may send a rushing beast.
sentences.s091-s16: I will send a certain daring, swift man to the vicinity of Afrasiab.
sentences.s091-s9: That the wakeful‑hearted king of Turan's army may remain forever with helmets.
sentences.s091-s10: From Zahhak our stock is Tazi (Arab); in this kingship I am half‑firmly glad.
sentences.s091-s11: By steadfast adherence I bought my life; besides this I saw no other remedy.
sentences.s091-s12: Now this house and seat are mine; that same Zāvolstān is also in my hand (under my control).
sentences.s091-s17: Unless he becomes aware of my secret, the speaker's words will be cut short.
sentences.s091-s18: I will send a prose message in the manner that is fitting; besides this, whatever comes from the royal court...
sentences.s091-s19: If then he says, 'Come to me,' I shall be nowhere except before his throne at his feet.
sentences.s091-s20: I will entrust the whole kingdom to you; always I will keep a glad heart for you.
sentences.s091-s25: That two champions came here to fight — an army of Turks like a leopard of the plain.
sentences.s091-s26: Two armies marched against the Helmand; I set their dinars at the foot, into a stanza.
sentences.s091-s27: If you speak of the coming even for a moment, the suspicious one's desire will immediately arise.
sentences.s091-s5: For the sake of (his) father Zal, with mourning and sorrow he made a tomb in Gūrāb.
sentences.s091-s6: Within the city there was a mihrab, which was bright‑souled and sleepless.
sentences.s091-s7: A messenger came from his presence; he turned his face toward the sexton Shamasas.
sentences.s091-s8: He went down before the inner curtain; from the mihrab it gave him abundant greetings.
